# Experiments {#sec-experiments}

Four controlled experiments, all using the frozen two-tower probe of
@sec-pipeline and the 4AFC metric on held-out children (chance 25%). Together
they test two levers against referential noise — *alignment filtering* and
*region grounding* — and probe how each behaves.

## B — The alignment gap is robust {#sec-b}

The base comparison holds data size fixed and varies only alignment: an
**aligned** arm (utterances with CLIP score $>0.24$) versus a **random** arm of
the same size drawn from the full distribution. Repeated across three held-out
children and three seeds each:

![Aligned vs. size-matched random, three held-out children (±sd over 3 seeds).
The gap is present and consistent everywhere.](figures/fig_results_B.png){#fig-b width=75%}

| Held-out child | aligned (>0.24) | random (matched) | gap |
|----------------|-----------------|------------------|-----|
| S00360001 | 46.9 ± 0.2 | 32.6 ± 1.6 | **+14.3** |
| S00240001 | 43.8 ± 0.6 | 33.6 ± 0.3 | **+10.1** |
| S00370002 | 44.5 ± 0.7 | 32.4 ± 0.3 | **+12.2** |
| **mean** | | | **+12.2** |

At identical data size, concentrating aligned pairs buys **+12 points**,
cross-child, with tight error bars. Alignment quality — not quantity — is doing
the work.

## A — Filtering beats brute scale {#sec-a}

Does more data help if it isn't filtered? We train on the **full unfiltered**
BabyView stream (1.14M pairs, held-out S00360001) and compare to the aligned and
random arms from @sec-b at that child.

![The full unfiltered stream (8× more data) underperforms the aligned subset it
contains.](figures/fig_results_A.png){#fig-a width=60%}

| Training set | pairs | best 4AFC |
|--------------|-------|-----------|
| aligned (clip>0.24) | 137k | **46.7** |
| unfiltered (everything) | 1.14M | 43.2 |
| random (size-matched) | 137k | 34.6 |

**Eight times more data is worse than the filtered subset it contains.** The
aligned pairs live *inside* the unfiltered stream; drowning them in ~1M
misaligned pairs dilutes the signal (46.7 → 43.2) but does not destroy it — the
unfiltered arm still beats random, because the good pairs are in there. Filtering
concentrates signal; scale alone dilutes it.

::: {.callout-note}
## An honest caveat about "divergence"

Prior reports of BabyView contrastive training describe the *loss climbing*
during end-to-end training. Our frozen-feature probe does **not** reproduce that:
its validation loss falls and then flattens (5.39 → 5.13, never rising). The
probe is too small to overfit 1.14M pairs, so the "loss goes up" phenomenon is an
optimization/capacity effect stacked on top of the alignment problem. Our probe
deliberately strips that away to isolate the alignment effect; the claim it
supports is "filtered > unfiltered," not "unfiltered diverges."
:::

## C — A count-vs-quality sweet spot {#sec-c}

If quality helps, does *more* quality always help? We sweep the filter threshold
at held-out S00360001. Higher thresholds mean cleaner but fewer pairs.

![Raising the threshold from 0.24 to 0.26 helps despite 6× fewer pairs; pushing
to 0.28 (only ~3.8k pairs) hurts.](figures/fig_results_C.png){#fig-c width=65%}

| min-clip | pairs | best 4AFC |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| 0.24 | 137,179 | 46.9 ± 0.2 |
| **0.26** | **21,877** | **48.4 ± 0.8** |
| 0.28 | 3,799 | 43.4 ± 0.7 |

The curve is **non-monotonic**: there is an optimum around 22k clean pairs.
Raising quality helps until it starves the model of data — at which point too few
pairs (0.28, 3.8k) cost more than the added purity buys. Notably the sweet spot
(~22k pairs) is about 2.4× SAYCam-S's 9,320 aligned pairs, and it *beats* using
6× more, slightly-noisier data. This is exactly the shape @vong2024robust's
"absolute count" thesis predicts: per-pair quality matters, but you still need
enough pairs.

## D — Object crops beat whole scenes {#sec-d}

The second lever: replace each frame with the crop of its dominant detected
object, so the model learns from the object rather than the cluttered scene.
Restricted to the 50k aligned pairs in detection-covered videos, we run the full
$2\times2$ of training representation × evaluation representation.

![Object crops beat whole scenes regardless of eval representation; crop/crop is
the best cell.](figures/fig_results_D.png){#fig-d width=55%}

|  | frame-eval | crop-eval |
|--|------------|-----------|
| **frame-train** | 39.2 | 45.4 |
| **crop-train** | 42.9 | **48.7** |

Reading across rows, **crop-training beats frame-training on the same eval**
(+3.7 on frame-eval, +3.3 on crop-eval): pairing the utterance with the isolated
object is a better learning signal than pairing with the scene. Evaluating on
crops also beats evaluating on frames (isolating the object at test time helps
too). The best cell — crop-train/crop-eval, **48.7** — is the highest number in
the study, on only 50k pairs.

(The frame-train/frame-eval baseline here, 39.2, is below the full aligned model
of @sec-b because it uses the smaller detection-video subset and pairs with the
detection frame rather than the CLIP-best frame; the clean comparison is crop vs.
frame on the *identical* 50k pairs.)

## Summary

| Experiment | Finding |
|------------|---------|
| B | Alignment filtering: **+12.2 pts** cross-child, size-matched, 3 seeds. |
| A | Filtered 137k **beats** unfiltered 1.14M — scale dilutes signal. |
| C | Count-vs-quality **sweet spot** at ~22k pairs (clip>0.26): 48.4. |
| D | Object crops beat whole scenes **+3–4 pts**; best overall 48.7. |

Two cheap, composable levers move word–object accuracy well above chance on a
corpus where naive training fails. The next chapter takes stock.
